title: Chapter 7 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on INSERTing, UPDATEing, DELETEing, and keeping your job!

questions.question: Which keyword officially inserts brand new completely fresh rows into a table?
questions.explanation: INSERT INTO writes distinct, totally new records from scratch into the database.

questions.question: When building an INSERT statement, immediately following the column list `(first_name, email)`, what keyword predictably precedes the actual injected data?
questions.explanation: Example: `INSERT INTO users (first_name) VALUES ('John');`

questions.question: True or False: The sequential order of the incoming VALUES must flawlessly match the exact sequence you declared your columns in.
questions.explanation: The database maps them strictly by position. If you say (age, name) but inject ('John', 25), the database crashes trying to stuff letters into an age field.

questions.question: To organically modify an illegally misspelled email address for an existing user, you utilize the _____ command.
questions.explanation: UPDATE alters the actual contents of an already existing row.

questions.question: Which crucial keyword flawlessly pairs with UPDATE to mechanically define exactly which columns to change, and what their new values will be?
questions.explanation: Example: `UPDATE users SET name = 'Bob'...`

questions.question: If you wish to simultaneously configure a user's `email` AND their `status` simultaneously within one single UPDATE block, how do you cleanly separate the targets?
options: By utilizing the AND keyword between them
By placing a comma between the declarations in the SET block
You physically cannot; it requires entirely separate queries
questions.explanation: `... SET email = 'a@b.com', status = 'Pro'...` Separable commas are the required syntax.

questions.question: What is the apocalyptic catastrophic consequence of deploying an UPDATE statement utterly lacking a restrictive WHERE clause?
options: It merely throws an annoying visual warning text
It irreversibly mutates every single solitary row in the entire collective table immediately
It automatically targets the earliest created row
It deletes the complete table structure inherently
questions.explanation: This is one of the most infamously dangerous beginner errors in programming history.

questions.question: Why is running an update like `WHERE last_name = 'Smith'` notoriously unsafe?
options: Because hundreds of people might inadvertently share that surname, simultaneously causing massive collateral overwrites
Because it is mathematically too slow
questions.explanation: Never update based on non-unique string identifiers unless you genuinely intend to alter masses of matching users universally.

questions.question: Surgical, microscopic precision in targeting an UPDATE query is safely achieved by exclusively evaluating the unique Primary _____ (like an ID number).
questions.explanation: A Primary Key (like user_id: 489) is mathematically guaranteed to be infinitely unique.

questions.question: Which keyword natively removes a row entirely from logical existence?
questions.explanation: DELETE obliterates targeted internal rows.

questions.question: True or False: Utilizing the DELETE command structurally destroys the entire physical database Table layout box itself.
questions.explanation: DELETE merely empties the targeted inner rows (data). The structured column framework box organically persists independently.

questions.question: What happens if you deploy `DELETE FROM users;` utterly bereft of an associated WHERE clause?
options: The computer inherently rejects the query
The entire table structure is visibly dismantled
Every single data row is successfully eradicated, leaving a completely hollow table behind
It simply deletes roughly half the rows
questions.explanation: Like UPDATE without a WHERE... DELETE without a WHERE is an absolute categorical nightmare.

questions.question: Because hard deleting is notoriously unrecoverable, modern architects frequently prefer running an UPDATE on a status column (like 'is_deleted = 1') to create a '_____ Delete'.
questions.explanation: A Soft Delete is a highly revered industry standard for preserving historical analytics whilst hiding front-end visibility.

questions.question: If you legitimately perform a 'Soft Delete', physically what SQL command genuinely ran beneath the surface?
questions.explanation: A Soft Delete is merely an UPDATE statement toggling a boolean flag. No literal deletion mechanics ever execute.

questions.question: Structurally, what belongs directly immediately after `DELETE FROM users` to keep your job secure?
questions.explanation: You MUST restrict the damage radius heavily using WHERE!

questions.question: Which properly formats a new item insertion?
questions.explanation: Table declaration -> Column target list -> VALUES statement -> Injected payload list.

questions.question: True or False: There is a native 'CTRL+Z' (Undo Command) structurally ingrained into basic standard single-query SQL actions.
questions.explanation: Single destructive queries run instantly. Once they commit... unless you've manually engineered complex transactional backups, it's genuinely gone.

questions.question: If `UPDATE inventory SET amount = amount + 5` executes with no WHERE... what mathematically results?
options: Every singular tracked item systematically increases its independent supply by exactly 5
Only the very largest item increases by 5
The inventory amount resets rigidly to merely 5 universally
questions.explanation: Without limits, it evaluates every row identically. It calculates `current amount + 5` universally.

questions.question: You invariably utilize the _____ clause to rigidly establish precisely *which* row merits catastrophic altering.
questions.explanation: WHERE evaluates condition states dynamically.

questions.question: Which of these natively constitutes 'Data Manipulation' (DML)?
questions.explanation: INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE purely manipulate internal data records directly.
